---
title: "Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / input directions
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # eigenvectors / principal axes
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second eigenvector / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second half of the **read-as-a-pair** fork
> that opened with [GEOM-15 *Diagonalization and the Eigendecomposition*], and the summit
> of **Movement IV — Eigenstructure and the Spectral Picture**. It assumes you can already
> diagonalize a matrix when it has a full set of independent eigenvectors, writing
> $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ and reading it as "change to the eigenbasis, scale, change back"
> ([GEOM-15]); that you know what an **orthonormal basis** is and why coordinates in one are
> just dot products ([GEOM-05]); and that you have met the **transpose** and the meaning of a
> matrix being **symmetric**, $A = A^\top$ ([GEOM-10]). The one question here: *why are
> symmetric matrices the best-behaved matrices in all of linear algebra, and what exactly
> does the spectral theorem promise?* It sets up [GEOM-17] (diagonalizing a quadratic form),
> [GEOM-18] (positive-definite $\Leftrightarrow$ positive eigenvalues), and [GEOM-20] (the
> SVD, *built* from the spectral decomposition of $A^\top A$). It is the engine behind
> **Unsupervised Learning's PCA** and **Inference's Fisher-information matrix**.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A general square matrix can be diagonalized only if it happens to have enough independent
eigenvectors, and even then its eigenvectors can sit at any awkward angles to one another
and its eigenvalues can be complex numbers. **Symmetric matrices escape every one of these
defects.** This article proves the **spectral theorem**: every real symmetric matrix $A$
has only **real** eigenvalues, and it has a full set of eigenvectors that are mutually
**orthogonal** — so it can always be written
$$A = Q\Lambda Q^\top,$$
where $Q$ is an *orthogonal* matrix (its columns are an orthonormal eigenbasis) and
$\Lambda$ is diagonal. We prove both halves — real eigenvalues, orthogonal eigenvectors —
from scratch, and read off the geometry: a symmetric matrix acts by *rotate to the
eigenaxes, stretch along each axis, rotate back* — **it never shears**. The single claim
this article defends is that this one factorization, available for free and only for
symmetric matrices, is the reason covariance matrices, Gram matrices $A^\top A$, and
Hessians are so tractable — it is the algebraic spine under PCA, quadratic forms, and
second-order optimization.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
For a general matrix, diagonalization $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ is a *privilege* — it needs a
full set of independent eigenvectors, and they can be skewed and complex. A real
**symmetric** matrix $A = A^\top$ is handed the perfect version of this for free: its
eigenvalues are all **real**, and its eigenvectors can be chosen **orthonormal**. Because
the eigenvectors are orthonormal, the change-of-basis matrix $V$ becomes an **orthogonal**
matrix $Q$, whose inverse is just its transpose, $Q^{-1} = Q^\top$. So the decomposition
collapses to
$$A = Q\Lambda Q^\top, \qquad Q^\top Q = I, \qquad \Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n).$$
Geometrically: **rotate to the eigenaxes** ($Q^\top$), **stretch along each axis** by its
eigenvalue ($\Lambda$), **rotate back** ($Q$). No shear, no rotation left over, no complex
arithmetic. This is the spectral theorem, and it is why the symmetric matrices that show up
all over data science — covariance, $A^\top A$, the Hessian — are the easy ones.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The two promises — *real eigenvalues* and *orthogonal
eigenvectors* — are not obvious, and we will earn each one. The payoff is that the most
important matrices a data scientist ever touches are exactly the symmetric ones, and the
spectral theorem says they are all, geometrically, the *same kind of object*: a set of
perpendicular axes, each with a stretch factor.

# Why we are here: the defects of general diagonalization {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **what goes wrong for a general matrix,
and why does symmetry fix every bit of it?** Every step below is an answer to some piece of
that question.

[GEOM-15] gave us the eigendecomposition $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$: collect $n$ independent
eigenvectors as the columns of $V$, put their eigenvalues on the diagonal of $\Lambda$, and
$A$ factors. That article was also honest about how *fragile* this is. Three separate things
can spoil it, and they are worth naming precisely, because the spectral theorem is exactly
the statement that symmetry kills all three at once.

**Defect 1 — there may not be enough eigenvectors (defectiveness).** The shear
$\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ has the single eigenvalue
$\lambda = 1$ repeated twice, but only *one* line of eigenvectors (the $x$-axis). It cannot
be diagonalized at all — there is no basis of eigenvectors to change into. Such a matrix is
called **defective**.

**Defect 2 — the eigenvalues may be complex.** A rotation by $90^\circ$,
$\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$, leaves *no* real direction
fixed (it turns everything), so its eigenvalues are $\pm i$. Diagonalization exists, but
only over the complex numbers — useless if you wanted real axes to interpret.

**Defect 3 — even when it works, the eigenvectors can be badly skewed.** Take
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
It is diagonalizable with eigenvalues $2$ and $5$, but its two eigenvectors are *not*
perpendicular. The change-of-basis matrix $V$ is then some general invertible matrix whose
inverse $V^{-1}$ is a genuine, expensive thing to compute, and "change to the eigenbasis"
means sliding into a slanted coordinate system.

@fig-three-defects shows all three failures side by side. Now contrast them with what we
are about to prove. For a symmetric matrix:

- it is **never defective** — a full orthonormal eigenbasis always exists;
- its eigenvalues are **always real** — no complex arithmetic, every eigenvalue is an
  honest stretch factor;
- its eigenvectors are **orthogonal** — the "slanted coordinate system" of Defect 3
  becomes a clean *rotation*, and $V^{-1}$ degenerates into the free, exact $Q^\top$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-defects
#| fig-cap: "The three ways general diagonalization fails, and the symmetric matrix that fixes all of them. **(a)** A shear is *defective*: it has only one eigendirection (red), so there is no eigenbasis. **(b)** A rotation has *no real* eigenvectors — every arrow (grey) is turned, none is merely scaled, so its eigenvalues are complex. **(c)** A non-symmetric diagonalizable matrix has *skewed* eigenvectors (red, green) sitting at an awkward angle. **(d)** A symmetric matrix: two *perpendicular* eigendirections (red, green), each simply stretched. The spectral theorem says (d) is the universal picture for symmetric matrices."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(11.0, 3.0))

def style(ax, title):
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-2.2, 2.2); ax.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
    ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)

# (a) shear: one eigendirection
ax = axes[0]; style(ax, "(a) shear: defective")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), (1.8, 0), color=HILITE, label="$v$", label_offset=(0, -0.3))
ax.text(0, -1.9, "only ONE\neigendirection", fontsize=8, ha="center", color=GREY)

# (b) rotation: no real eigenvectors
ax = axes[1]; style(ax, "(b) rotation: complex")
R = np.array([[0, -1], [1, 0]])   # 90-degree rotation: no real eigenvector
for ang in np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 8, endpoint=False):
    v = 1.6*np.array([np.cos(ang), np.sin(ang)])      # an input direction
    w = R @ v                                          # where the rotation sends it
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(v), color=PALE, lw=1.2, z=2)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(w), color=GREY, lw=1.2, z=1)
ax.text(0, -1.9, "every arrow\nis turned", fontsize=8, ha="center", color=GREY)

# (c) non-symmetric diagonalizable: skew eigenvectors
ax = axes[2]; style(ax, "(c) skewed eigenbasis")
v1 = np.array([1.0, 0.0]); v2 = np.array([3.0, 3.0]); v2 = v2/np.linalg.norm(v2)*1.8
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(v1*1.8), color=HILITE, label="$v_1$", label_offset=(0.1, -0.3))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(v2), color=ACCENT, label="$v_2$", label_offset=(0.3, 0.1))
ax.text(0, -1.9, "not perpendicular", fontsize=8, ha="center", color=GREY)

# (d) symmetric: orthogonal eigenvectors
ax = axes[3]; style(ax, "(d) symmetric: orthogonal")
q1 = np.array([np.cos(np.deg2rad(30)), np.sin(np.deg2rad(30))])
q2 = np.array([-q1[1], q1[0]])
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(1.8*q1), color=HILITE, label="$q_1$", label_offset=(0.2, -0.2))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(1.8*q2), color=ACCENT, label="$q_2$", label_offset=(-0.3, 0.1))
ax.text(0, -1.9, "perpendicular axes", fontsize=8, ha="center", color=GREY)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Diagonalization already exists for lots of matrices — what does symmetry actually buy
me?"* Three concrete things, and you should give all three. **(1) Guaranteed
diagonalizability:** you never have to check whether enough eigenvectors exist — they
always do. **(2) Real, interpretable eigenvalues:** every eigenvalue is a real stretch
factor you can read as variance, curvature, or energy, never a complex number. **(3) The
inverse is free and exact:** because the eigenvectors are orthonormal, the change-of-basis
matrix is *orthogonal*, so $V^{-1} = V^\top$ — no matrix inversion, no loss of precision,
and the geometry is a rigid rotation rather than a shear. The first two are about
*existence*; the third is the one that makes the symmetric case *computationally and
geometrically clean*, and it is the reason the SVD ([GEOM-20]) is built on top of it.
:::

# Intuition before symbols: a symmetric matrix is a set of stretches on perpendicular axes {#sec-intuition}

Before any proof, here is the picture the theorem will make rigorous. Take a symmetric
$2\times 2$ matrix and watch what it does to the unit circle — to *every* unit vector at
once. A general matrix maps the circle to a tilted, sheared ellipse whose "axes" need not
be perpendicular. A *symmetric* matrix maps the circle to an ellipse whose axes **are**
perpendicular, and those axes point exactly along the eigenvectors, stretched by the
eigenvalues. @fig-ellipse shows it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ellipse
#| fig-cap: 'A symmetric matrix turns the unit circle into an ellipse whose axes are *perpendicular* and aligned with the eigenvectors. **Left:** the unit circle (grey) with the two orthonormal eigenvectors $q_1, q_2$ (red, green). **Right:** the image $A x$ as $x$ runs around the circle (blue ellipse). The eigenvectors do not rotate — they only stretch, by $\\lambda_1$ and $\\lambda_2$ — so they land exactly on the ellipse''s axes. There is no shear: the right angle between $q_1$ and $q_2$ is preserved. This is the entire geometric content of the spectral theorem.'
A = np.array([[3.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])   # symmetric
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(A)
q1, q2 = V[:, 0], V[:, 1]
lam1, lam2 = w

t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 400)
circle = np.vstack([np.cos(t), np.sin(t)])
ellipse = A @ circle

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))
for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
    ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)

axL.plot(circle[0], circle[1], color=GREY, lw=1.5)
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), tuple(q1), color=HILITE, label="$q_1$", label_offset=(0.15, 0.2))
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), tuple(q2), color=ACCENT, label="$q_2$", label_offset=(0.2, 0.15))
axL.set_xlim(-1.5, 1.5); axL.set_ylim(-1.5, 1.5)
axL.set_title("unit circle + eigenvectors")

axR.plot(ellipse[0], ellipse[1], color=VEC, lw=2.0)
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), tuple(lam1*q1), color=HILITE, label=fr"$\lambda_1 q_1$",
         label_offset=(0.4, 0.4))
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), tuple(lam2*q2), color=ACCENT, label=fr"$\lambda_2 q_2$",
         label_offset=(0.5, 0.3))
axR.set_xlim(-4.5, 4.5); axR.set_ylim(-4.5, 4.5)
axR.set_title(fr"image $Ax$: axes at $\lambda_1={lam1:.2f},\ \lambda_2={lam2:.2f}$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Why should symmetry produce perpendicular axes? Here is the intuition the algebra will
confirm. A symmetric matrix has no "preferred handedness" — the influence of coordinate
$i$ on coordinate $j$ (the entry $A_{ij}$) equals the influence of $j$ on $i$ (the entry
$A_{ji}$). That mutual, balanced coupling is exactly what forbids a shear (a shear is
one-sided: it pushes $x$ in the direction of $y$ but not the reverse). With no shear left
to perform, all the matrix *can* do is stretch along some axes — and the balance forces
those axes to be perpendicular. The rest of the article turns "balance forbids shear" into
two clean theorems.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We will need two pieces of named machinery from earlier in the track, plus a definition.
We state them, recall why they are true, and then prove the theorem.

## What "symmetric" and "orthogonal" mean, precisely {#sec-defs}

::: {#def-symmetric}
**Definition (symmetric matrix).** A real $n\times n$ matrix $A$ is **symmetric** if it
equals its own transpose, $A = A^\top$ — equivalently, $A_{ij} = A_{ji}$ for every pair
$i,j$. The matrix is mirror-symmetric across its main diagonal.
:::

::: {#def-orthogonal}
**Definition (orthogonal matrix).** A real $n\times n$ matrix $Q$ is **orthogonal** if its
columns are orthonormal — each column is a unit vector and any two distinct columns are
perpendicular. In one equation,
$$
Q^\top Q = I .
$$ {#eq-orth}
Because $Q$ is square, @eq-orth also forces $Q Q^\top = I$, so $Q^{-1} = Q^\top$. An
orthogonal matrix is a *rigid motion*: it preserves all lengths and angles (a rotation,
possibly with a reflection), which is why it can only "rotate to new axes," never stretch.
:::

We should not take @eq-orth on faith — let us see *why* "columns orthonormal" is the same
statement as $Q^\top Q = I$, since the whole theorem hinges on it. Write $Q$ with columns
$q_1,\dots,q_n$. The $(i,j)$ entry of $Q^\top Q$ is the dot product of the $i$-th row of
$Q^\top$ with the $j$-th column of $Q$ — and the $i$-th row of $Q^\top$ *is* the $i$-th
column $q_i$ of $Q$. So
$$
\bigl(Q^\top Q\bigr)_{ij} = q_i^\top q_j .
$$
"Orthonormal columns" means $q_i^\top q_j = 1$ when $i=j$ (unit length) and $q_i^\top q_j =
0$ when $i\neq j$ (perpendicular) — which is precisely the statement that $Q^\top Q$ has
$1$'s on the diagonal and $0$'s off it, i.e. $Q^\top Q = I$. (This unpacking of $Q^\top Q$
as the matrix of all pairwise dot products of columns is exactly the Gram-matrix reading
introduced in [GEOM-10]; here it does the central bookkeeping.)

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does $Q^{-1} = Q^\top$ matter so much?"* Because the eigendecomposition $A = V\Lambda
V^{-1}$ needs the inverse of the eigenvector matrix, and inverting a general matrix is
costly, numerically delicate (it can lose precision badly for ill-conditioned $V$;
[GEOM-26]), and geometrically a *shear*. When $V$ is orthogonal, its inverse is a free
transpose — flip rows and columns, done exactly — and the operation it performs is a rigid
rotation that preserves all the geometry. This single fact, $Q^{-1} = Q^\top$, is what makes
the symmetric case the one you actually want to compute with.
:::

## The transpose identity we will lean on {#sec-transpose-tool}

The proof of real eigenvalues rests on one identity, derived in [GEOM-10] and recalled here
because it is the hinge of the argument. For any matrices of conformable sizes,
$$
(AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top .
$$ {#eq-transpose-product}
A two-line reminder of why: the $(i,j)$ entry of $AB$ is row $i$ of $A$ dotted with column
$j$ of $B$; transposing swaps the roles of $i$ and $j$, giving row $j$ of $A$ dotted with
column $i$ of $B$, which is exactly the $(i,j)$ entry of $B^\top A^\top$ (row $i$ of
$B^\top$ is column $i$ of $B$; column $j$ of $A^\top$ is row $j$ of $A$). One immediate
consequence we use constantly: a dot product is a $1\times 1$ matrix, equal to its own
transpose, so for column vectors $u, v$,
$$
u^\top v = (u^\top v)^\top = v^\top u .
$$ {#eq-dot-symmetric}

We will also use the **adjoint move**: for *symmetric* $A$, a factor of $A$ can hop from one
side of a dot product to the other for free. Concretely, for any vectors $x, y$,
$$
(Ax)^\top y \;\overset{\text{transpose}}{=}\; x^\top A^\top y
\;\overset{A = A^\top}{=}\; x^\top (A y) .
$$ {#eq-adjoint}
In words: $\langle Ax, y\rangle = \langle x, Ay\rangle$ — for a symmetric matrix you may
move $A$ across the dot product without changing anything. This single sentence does almost
all the work in both halves of the spectral theorem.

## Half one: the eigenvalues are real {#sec-real-eigenvalues}

A general real matrix can have complex eigenvalues (the rotation of @fig-three-defects). We
prove a real *symmetric* matrix cannot. The argument needs the **complex conjugate**, so we
recall it from the floor: for a complex number $z = a + bi$ (with $a,b$ real and
$i^2=-1$), the conjugate is $\bar z = a - bi$, and the product $z\bar z = a^2 + b^2 = |z|^2$
is a *nonnegative real number*, zero only when $z=0$. We extend the bar to vectors and
matrices entrywise: $\bar v$ conjugates every component of $v$, and $\bar A$ conjugates every
entry of $A$.

Two facts about the bar we will use, both true entry by entry: it distributes over products,
$\overline{Mw} = \bar M\,\bar w$, and it leaves *real* quantities alone, $\bar A = A$ when
$A$ is real. The matrix in our theorem is real, so $\bar A = A$ — hold onto that.

::: {#thm-real}
**Theorem (real eigenvalues).** Every eigenvalue of a real symmetric matrix $A = A^\top$ is
a real number.
:::

::: {.proof}
Let $\lambda$ be any eigenvalue of $A$, with a (possibly complex) eigenvector $v\neq 0$:
$$
A v = \lambda v .
$$
Our goal is to show $\lambda = \bar\lambda$, because a number equal to its own conjugate has
zero imaginary part and so is real ($a+bi = a-bi$ forces $b=0$).

The right tool is the quantity $\bar v^\top A v$, a single number we will compute two ways
and set equal. **First computation.** Use the eigenvalue equation directly:
$$
\bar v^\top A v = \bar v^\top (\lambda v) = \lambda\,(\bar v^\top v) .
$$ {#eq-real-first}
Here $\bar v^\top v = \sum_k \overline{v_k}\, v_k = \sum_k |v_k|^2$ is a sum of squared
magnitudes — a *strictly positive real number*, because $v\neq 0$ means at least one
component is nonzero. Call it $\|v\|^2 > 0$.

**Second computation.** Take the conjugate-transpose of the eigenvalue equation and feed it
back in. Start from $A v = \lambda v$. Conjugate both sides entrywise; since $A$ is real
($\bar A = A$),
$$
\overline{A v} = \bar A\,\bar v = A\,\bar v, \qquad \overline{\lambda v} = \bar\lambda\,\bar v,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad A\,\bar v = \bar\lambda\,\bar v .
$$
Now transpose this relation and use @eq-transpose-product on the left, recalling
$A^\top = A$:
$$
(A\,\bar v)^\top = \bar v^\top A^\top = \bar v^\top A,
\qquad
(\bar\lambda\,\bar v)^\top = \bar\lambda\,\bar v^\top,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
\bar v^\top A = \bar\lambda\,\bar v^\top .
$$
Multiply this on the right by $v$:
$$
\bar v^\top A v = \bar\lambda\,(\bar v^\top v) = \bar\lambda\,\|v\|^2 .
$$ {#eq-real-second}

**Set them equal.** The left-hand sides of @eq-real-first and @eq-real-second are the *same
number* $\bar v^\top A v$, so the right-hand sides agree:
$$
\lambda\,\|v\|^2 = \bar\lambda\,\|v\|^2 .
$$
Since $\|v\|^2 > 0$ we may divide it out, leaving
$$
\lambda = \bar\lambda .
$$
A complex number equal to its own conjugate is real. Hence every eigenvalue $\lambda$ is
real. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The engine of that proof was @eq-adjoint — we slid $A$ from acting on $v$ (first
computation) to acting on $\bar v$ (second), and symmetry made the two readings of
$\bar v^\top A v$ identical. A non-symmetric matrix would have produced $A^\top$ in the
second computation, $A^\top \neq A$, and the two sides would *not* match — which is exactly
why a rotation gets to have complex eigenvalues.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where in that proof did symmetry actually get used?"* In exactly one place: the step
$\bar v^\top A^\top = \bar v^\top A$, which needs $A^\top = A$. Strip out symmetry and the
second computation produces $\bar v^\top A^\top v = \bar\lambda\|v\|^2$, with $A^\top$, not
$A$ — and you can no longer equate it to the first computation's $\bar v^\top A v$. The
whole "eigenvalues are real" guarantee lives or dies on that one substitution.
:::

## Half two: eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal {#sec-orthogonal-eigvecs}

Now the geometric half. We show that eigenvectors belonging to *different* eigenvalues are
automatically perpendicular — no choice involved, symmetry forces it.

::: {#thm-orthogonal}
**Theorem (orthogonal eigenvectors).** Let $A = A^\top$ be real symmetric. If $A v_1 =
\lambda_1 v_1$ and $A v_2 = \lambda_2 v_2$ with $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$, then
$v_1^\top v_2 = 0$: the eigenvectors are orthogonal.
:::

::: {.proof}
By @thm-real both eigenvalues are real, so there is no conjugation to worry about. Compute
the single number $v_1^\top A v_2$ two ways, using the adjoint move @eq-adjoint.

**Let $A$ act to the right** (on $v_2$):
$$
v_1^\top A v_2 = v_1^\top (\lambda_2 v_2) = \lambda_2\,(v_1^\top v_2) .
$$
**Let $A$ act to the left** (on $v_1$), legal because $A$ is symmetric — this is exactly
@eq-adjoint with $x = v_1,\ y = v_2$:
$$
v_1^\top A v_2 = (A v_1)^\top v_2 = (\lambda_1 v_1)^\top v_2 = \lambda_1\,(v_1^\top v_2) .
$$
(The first equality regroups $v_1^\top A v_2$ as $(v_1^\top A)\,v_2$ and rewrites the
left factor: $v_1^\top A = v_1^\top A^\top = (A v_1)^\top$, using $A^\top = A$ and the
transpose rule $(A v_1)^\top = v_1^\top A^\top$ from @eq-transpose-product. Nothing was
dropped — $A$ simply moved from acting on $v_2$ on the right to acting on $v_1$ on the
left, which is the content of @eq-adjoint.) The two results are the same
number, so
$$
\lambda_2\,(v_1^\top v_2) = \lambda_1\,(v_1^\top v_2)
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
(\lambda_2 - \lambda_1)\,(v_1^\top v_2) = 0 .
$$
Because $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$, the factor $\lambda_2 - \lambda_1$ is nonzero, so the
*other* factor must vanish:
$$
v_1^\top v_2 = 0 .
$$
The eigenvectors are orthogonal. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That is the second pillar. Notice it is the same trick as the first proof — push $A$ across
a dot product using symmetry — applied to two different eigenvectors instead of one
eigenvector and its conjugate. Symmetry is the only ingredient.

## What about repeated eigenvalues? {#sec-repeated}

@thm-orthogonal covers *distinct* eigenvalues. But a symmetric matrix can repeat an
eigenvalue — the identity $I$ has $\lambda = 1$ with multiplicity $n$. We must rule out the
defectiveness of @fig-three-defects(a): we need to know that a repeated eigenvalue of a
*symmetric* matrix still supplies a full set of eigenvectors (an **eigenspace** of the right
dimension), and that within that eigenspace we can *choose* an orthonormal set.

The good news is that the second worry is free: any vector in the $\lambda$-eigenspace is an
eigenvector for $\lambda$ (if $Av = \lambda v$ and $Aw = \lambda w$ then $A(av+bw) =
\lambda(av+bw)$ — the eigenspace is a subspace), and **any** basis of a subspace can be
turned into an orthonormal one spanning the *same* subspace by **Gram–Schmidt** ([GEOM-07]).
So *within* one eigenspace orthonormality is just bookkeeping. And eigenvectors from
*different* eigenspaces are already orthogonal by @thm-orthogonal. The only real content
left is: does a symmetric matrix actually supply enough eigenvectors to fill the space — is
it never defective? It is not, and the clean way to see it is to build the whole orthonormal
eigenbasis at once by induction.

::: {#thm-spectral}
**Spectral theorem (real symmetric matrices).** Every real symmetric $n\times n$ matrix $A$
can be written
$$
A = Q\Lambda Q^\top ,
$$ {#eq-spectral}
where $Q$ is orthogonal ($Q^\top Q = I$) and $\Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,
\dots,\lambda_n)$ is real diagonal. The columns $q_1,\dots,q_n$ of $Q$ are an orthonormal
basis of eigenvectors, with $A q_k = \lambda_k q_k$. Equivalently, $A$ is a sum of rank-one
projections onto perpendicular directions,
$$
A = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \lambda_k\, q_k q_k^\top .
$$ {#eq-spectral-outer}
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the existence of an orthonormal eigenbasis by induction on $n$; the matrix forms
@eq-spectral and @eq-spectral-outer then follow by pure bookkeeping, shown at the end.

**Base case $n=1$.** A $1\times 1$ matrix $[a]$ is its own eigenvalue with eigenvector
$[1]$, already orthonormal. Done.

**Inductive step.** Assume every real symmetric $(n-1)\times(n-1)$ matrix has an orthonormal
eigenbasis; we produce one for the $n\times n$ case. Three named facts get us there, each
already established or recalled:

*1. $A$ has at least one real eigenvalue with a real eigenvector.* The eigenvalues are the
roots of the characteristic polynomial $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ ([GEOM-14]). A degree-$n$
real polynomial has at least one root over the complex numbers (this is the **Fundamental
Theorem of Algebra**, a result about polynomials we import as a known fact — it is below our
concern here and proved in any complex-analysis course). By @thm-real that root is *real*.
Call it $\lambda_1$. For a real $\lambda_1$, the matrix $A - \lambda_1 I$ is real and
singular (its determinant is zero), so its null space ([GEOM-12]) contains a nonzero *real*
vector; normalize it to a unit eigenvector $q_1$, with $A q_1 = \lambda_1 q_1$ and
$\|q_1\| = 1$.

*2. The subspace perpendicular to $q_1$ is preserved by $A$.* Let
$$
W = \{\, w : q_1^\top w = 0 \,\}
$$
be the **orthogonal complement** of $q_1$ — all vectors perpendicular to $q_1$, an
$(n-1)$-dimensional subspace ([GEOM-05]). Claim: if $w \in W$ then $Aw \in W$ too (we say
$W$ is *$A$-invariant*). To check it, we must show $q_1^\top (Aw) = 0$. Use the adjoint move
@eq-adjoint to slide $A$ onto $q_1$, then use that $q_1$ is an eigenvector:
$$
q_1^\top (A w) = (A q_1)^\top w = (\lambda_1 q_1)^\top w = \lambda_1\,(q_1^\top w)
= \lambda_1 \cdot 0 = 0 .
$$
So $Aw$ is again perpendicular to $q_1$, i.e. $Aw \in W$. This is the crucial place
symmetry pays off: *for a symmetric matrix, the orthogonal complement of an eigenvector is
invariant*, so we can legitimately restrict $A$ to $W$ and recurse. (For a non-symmetric
matrix this fails — there is no reason $Aw$ stays perpendicular to $q_1$ — which is the
deep reason non-symmetric matrices can be defective.)

*3. The restriction of $A$ to $W$ is again symmetric.* Build an orthonormal basis
$u_2,\dots,u_n$ of the $(n-1)$-dimensional space $W$ (Gram–Schmidt, [GEOM-07]), and let $B$
be the matrix of "apply $A$, expressed in this basis": its $(i,j)$ entry is $B_{ij} =
u_i^\top A u_j$ (the $i$-th coordinate of $A u_j$ in the orthonormal $u$-basis is the dot
product $u_i^\top (A u_j)$, by the orthonormal-coordinates fact of [GEOM-05]). Is $B$
symmetric? Check $B_{ji}$ against $B_{ij}$ with the adjoint move once more:
$$
B_{ji} = u_j^\top A u_i = (A u_j)^\top u_i = u_i^\top (A u_j) = B_{ij},
$$
using @eq-dot-symmetric for the middle equality ($(Au_j)^\top u_i = u_i^\top (Au_j)$, both
being the same scalar) and @eq-adjoint to move $A$. So $B = B^\top$: the restricted map is
an $(n-1)\times(n-1)$ *symmetric* matrix.

*Apply the inductive hypothesis.* By induction, $B$ has an orthonormal eigenbasis
$z_2,\dots,z_n$ inside $W$, with $A z_k = \mu_k z_k$ for real eigenvalues $\mu_k$. (These
$z_k$ are eigenvectors of the *original* $A$ acting on $W$, because $B$ is just $A$ written
in the $u$-basis.) Together with $q_1$, the set
$$
q_1,\; z_2,\; \dots,\; z_n
$$
is orthonormal: each $z_k \in W$ is perpendicular to $q_1$ by construction of $W$, the
$z_k$ are mutually orthonormal by the inductive hypothesis, and $q_1$ is a unit vector. That
is $n$ orthonormal eigenvectors of $A$ — a full orthonormal eigenbasis. The induction is
complete.

**From the eigenbasis to $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$.** Assemble the orthonormal eigenvectors as
the columns of $Q = [\,q_1\ \cdots\ q_n\,]$ and their eigenvalues as $\Lambda =
\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n)$. The $n$ eigenvalue equations $A q_k =
\lambda_k q_k$ stacked side by side say, column by column,
$$
A Q = A[\,q_1\ \cdots\ q_n\,] = [\,\lambda_1 q_1\ \cdots\ \lambda_n q_n\,] = Q\Lambda ,
$$
where the last equality holds because right-multiplying $Q$ by the diagonal $\Lambda$ scales
its $k$-th column by $\lambda_k$. Now right-multiply $AQ = Q\Lambda$ by $Q^\top$ and use
$Q Q^\top = I$ (orthogonality, @eq-orth):
$$
A = A Q Q^\top = Q\Lambda Q^\top .
$$
**The outer-product form.** Expand $Q\Lambda Q^\top$ as a sum. Writing it out, $Q\Lambda$
has columns $\lambda_k q_k$, and multiplying by $Q^\top$ (whose rows are the $q_k^\top$)
gives the block/outer-product expansion (the "sum of column-times-row" reading of matrix
multiplication from [GEOM-09]):
$$
A = Q\Lambda Q^\top = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \lambda_k\, q_k q_k^\top ,
$$
which is @eq-spectral-outer. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrowed, and where it is built"}
This proof imported exactly one ingredient from outside the Geometry track: the
**Fundamental Theorem of Algebra** (every nonconstant polynomial has a complex root), used
only to guarantee that the characteristic polynomial has *some* root, which @thm-real then
forces to be real. Everything else — the characteristic polynomial ([GEOM-14]), null spaces
([GEOM-12]), orthogonal complements and orthonormal coordinates ([GEOM-05]), Gram–Schmidt
([GEOM-07]), the transpose identity ([GEOM-10]), and the outer-product reading of a product
([GEOM-09]) — lives inside this track and was recalled where used. We flag the FTA rather
than re-prove it because it is an analysis result, genuinely outside the linear-algebra
floor; its statement is all the spectral theorem needs.
:::

## Reading the decomposition three ways {#sec-three-readings}

The single equation @eq-spectral repays being read in three complementary ways. Each is a
sentence you should be able to say out loud.

**As a change of coordinates.** $A x = Q\Lambda Q^\top x$ says: take $x$, express it in the
eigenbasis by computing its coordinates $Q^\top x$ (each coordinate is a dot product $q_k^\top
x$, by [GEOM-05]); scale the $k$-th coordinate by $\lambda_k$ via $\Lambda$; reassemble in the
standard basis with $Q$. *Rotate, stretch, rotate back.*

**As a sum of projections.** @eq-spectral-outer writes $A = \sum_k \lambda_k q_k q_k^\top$.
Each $q_k q_k^\top$ is the matrix that **projects** any vector onto the line through $q_k$
(applied to $x$ it returns $(q_k^\top x)\,q_k$, the component of $x$ along $q_k$; this is the
single-vector projection of [GEOM-06], and indeed $q_k q_k^\top$ is symmetric and idempotent).
So $A$ takes a vector apart into its components along the perpendicular eigenaxes, multiplies
the $k$-th component by $\lambda_k$, and adds the pieces back. A symmetric matrix *is* a
weighted sum of perpendicular projections.

**As an action on the unit sphere.** Because $Q$ is a rigid rotation, the only shape-changing
step is $\Lambda$, which stretches axis $k$ by $\lambda_k$. So $A$ maps the unit sphere to an
**ellipsoid** whose semi-axes point along $q_1,\dots,q_n$ with lengths $|\lambda_1|,\dots,
|\lambda_n|$ — exactly @fig-ellipse, now understood as a theorem. A negative $\lambda_k$ flips
that axis; a zero $\lambda_k$ flattens it (the matrix is singular, collapsing that direction).

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**$Q\Lambda Q^\top$ is not the same as $Q^\top\Lambda Q$, and the order of $Q$'s columns must
match the order of $\Lambda$'s diagonal.** Two traps catch people. First, the eigenvector
matrix sits on the *left* and its transpose on the *right* in @eq-spectral; swapping them
computes a *different* matrix (it diagonalizes in the wrong direction). Second, the $k$-th
column of $Q$ must be the eigenvector of the $k$-th diagonal entry of $\Lambda$ — if you sort
the eigenvalues (as `numpy.linalg.eigh` does, ascending) you must carry the eigenvectors
along in the same permutation, or the product silently reconstructs the wrong matrix. The
reconstruction check `Q @ Lambda @ Q.T` should return $A$ to machine precision; if it does
not, your pairing or your transpose is off.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the theorem on real, messy matrices — never one rigged to flatter it. The first we
do entirely by hand so you watch every promise of the theorem come true on the page; the
later ones use the symmetric matrices a data scientist actually meets, including one at the
honest edge of the theorem's reach.

## Example 1 — a $2\times 2$ symmetric matrix, by hand {#sec-ex-byhand}

Take the symmetric matrix
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad A = A^\top .
$$
**Eigenvalues.** Form the characteristic polynomial $\det(A - \lambda I)$ ([GEOM-14]). With
$A - \lambda I = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 4-\lambda & 1 \\ 1 & 4-\lambda \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$,
the $2\times2$ determinant (product of the diagonal minus product of the off-diagonal) is
$$
\det(A - \lambda I) = (4-\lambda)^2 - (1)(1) = (4-\lambda)^2 - 1 .
$$
Set it to zero: $(4-\lambda)^2 = 1$, so $4 - \lambda = \pm 1$, giving
$$
\lambda_1 = 5, \qquad \lambda_2 = 3 .
$$
Both **real**, as @thm-real promised. **Eigenvectors.** For $\lambda_1 = 5$, solve
$(A - 5I)v = 0$:
$$
A - 5I = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \end{bmatrix} = 0
\;\Longrightarrow\; -v_a + v_b = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; v_a = v_b .
$$
So the eigenvector points along $(1,1)$; normalize to unit length (divide by $\sqrt{1^2+1^2}
= \sqrt2$):
$$
q_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} .
$$
For $\lambda_2 = 3$, solve $(A - 3I)v = 0$:
$$
A - 3I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\;\Longrightarrow\; v_a + v_b = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; v_a = -v_b,
\qquad
q_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} .
$$
**Check orthogonality** (@thm-orthogonal, no choice involved):
$$
q_1^\top q_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\,(1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot(-1))
= \frac12(1 - 1) = 0 . \checkmark
$$
**Assemble and reconstruct.** With
$$
Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix},
$$
we verify $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ by hand. First $Q\Lambda$ scales the columns of $Q$ by $5$
and $3$:
$$
Q\Lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 3 \\ 5 & -3 \end{bmatrix} .
$$
Then multiply by $Q^\top = \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1
\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$:
$$
Q\Lambda Q^\top
= \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt2}
\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 3 \\ 5 & -3 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
= \frac12 \begin{bmatrix} 5+3 & 5-3 \\ 5-3 & 5+3 \end{bmatrix}
= \frac12\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 2 \\ 2 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = A . \checkmark
$$
Every promise of the theorem held: real eigenvalues, perpendicular unit eigenvectors, and an
exact reconstruction with $Q^{-1}$ replaced for free by $Q^\top$. The code below confirms it
and draws the geometry.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-byhand
#| fig-cap: 'The hand-worked example $A = \\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}4&1\\\\1&4\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$. The eigenvectors $q_1=(1,1)/\\sqrt2$ (red) and $q_2=(1,-1)/\\sqrt2$ (green) are perpendicular; $A$ stretches along $q_1$ by $\\lambda_1=5$ and along $q_2$ by $\\lambda_2=3$, turning the unit circle (grey) into an ellipse (blue) whose axes lie exactly on the eigenvectors. The diagonal symmetry of $A$ shows up as the $45^\\circ$ tilt of the principal axis.'
A = np.array([[4.0, 1.0], [1.0, 4.0]])
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(A)                 # ascending: lambda=3 then 5
order = np.argsort(w)[::-1]              # put largest first for plotting
w, V = w[order], V[:, order]
q1, q2 = V[:, 0], V[:, 1]

t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 400)
circ = np.vstack([np.cos(t), np.sin(t)])
ell = A @ circ

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.2))
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
ax.plot(circ[0], circ[1], color=GREY, lw=1.5, label="unit circle")
ax.plot(ell[0], ell[1], color=VEC, lw=2.0, label="image $Ax$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(w[0]*q1), color=HILITE, label=fr"$\lambda_1 q_1$", label_offset=(0.6, 0.5))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(w[1]*q2), color=ACCENT, label=fr"$\lambda_2 q_2$", label_offset=(0.7, -0.4))
ax.set_xlim(-6, 6); ax.set_ylim(-6, 6)
ax.set_title(fr"$\lambda_1={w[0]:.0f},\ \lambda_2={w[1]:.0f}$; eigenvectors $\perp$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

Q = V; Lam = np.diag(w)
recon = Q @ Lam @ Q.T
print("Q^T Q  =\n", np.round(Q.T @ Q, 12), "   (= I, columns orthonormal)")
print("max |A - Q Lambda Q^T| =", f"{np.max(np.abs(A - recon)):.2e}", "  (exact to machine precision)")
```

## Example 2 — a covariance matrix and the geometry of a data cloud {#sec-ex-covariance}

The symmetric matrix a data scientist meets most often is a **covariance matrix**. (Its
probabilistic meaning — how features co-vary — is owned by the Probability track, PROB-17;
here we use it only as a concrete *symmetric* matrix and read its geometry.) For two centered
features, the sample covariance matrix is
$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X^\top X
= \begin{bmatrix} \operatorname{Var}(x_1) & \operatorname{Cov}(x_1,x_2) \\
                  \operatorname{Cov}(x_1,x_2) & \operatorname{Var}(x_2) \end{bmatrix},
$$
symmetric *by construction* because $\operatorname{Cov}(x_1,x_2) = \operatorname{Cov}(x_2,x_1)$
(equivalently, $X^\top X$ is always symmetric — see [GEOM-10]). The spectral theorem hands us
the **principal axes** of the cloud: the eigenvectors $q_1,q_2$ are the perpendicular
directions of greatest and least spread, and the eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2$ are the
variances along them. This is the linear-algebra heart of PCA; the *statistical* procedure
(centering, choosing components, interpreting variance) is Unsupervised Learning's to own,
and it links back here for exactly this decomposition.

```{python}
#| label: fig-covariance
#| fig-cap: 'A correlated data cloud and the principal axes the spectral theorem extracts from its covariance matrix. The points (blue) have correlated $x_1,x_2$. Diagonalizing $\\Sigma = Q\\Lambda Q^\\top$ gives perpendicular eigenvectors $q_1$ (red, direction of maximum variance) and $q_2$ (green, minimum variance); the arrows are scaled by $\\sqrt{\\lambda_k}$ (one standard deviation along each axis). The dashed ellipse is the one-standard-deviation contour $\\{x : x^\\top \\Sigma^{-1} x = 1\\}$ — its axes are exactly the eigenvectors. The eigenstructure of $\\Sigma$ *is* the shape of the cloud.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
# A genuinely correlated cloud: draw from a known covariance.
true_cov = np.array([[3.0, 1.6], [1.6, 1.2]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(true_cov)
pts = (L @ rng.normal(size=(2, 300))).T
pts = pts - pts.mean(axis=0)
Sigma = np.cov(pts, rowvar=False)
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(Sigma)
order = np.argsort(w)[::-1]; w, V = w[order], V[:, order]
q1, q2 = V[:, 0], V[:, 1]

th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
# one-sigma ellipse: x = Q diag(sqrt(lambda)) [cos,sin]
ell = V @ np.diag(np.sqrt(w)) @ np.vstack([np.cos(th), np.sin(th)])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 5.2))
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], s=14, color=PALE, alpha=0.8, label="data")
ax.plot(ell[0], ell[1], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=r"1-$\sigma$ ellipse")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(np.sqrt(w[0])*q1), color=HILITE,
         label=r"$\sqrt{\lambda_1}\,q_1$", label_offset=(0.5, 0.5))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(np.sqrt(w[1])*q2), color=ACCENT,
         label=r"$\sqrt{\lambda_2}\,q_2$", label_offset=(-0.8, 0.5))
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.4); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.4)
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$ (centered)"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$ (centered)")
ax.set_title("Principal axes = eigenvectors of the covariance")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print("Sigma =\n", np.round(Sigma, 3))
print(f"eigenvalues (variances along axes): {w[0]:.3f}, {w[1]:.3f}")
print(f"q1 (max-variance direction): ({q1[0]:.3f}, {q1[1]:.3f})")
print(f"q1 . q2 = {q1 @ q2:.2e}  (orthogonal)")
```

The eigenvalues are the variances along the principal directions and the eigenvectors are
those directions — perpendicular, as the theorem guarantees, so PCA's "uncorrelated
components" is not an assumption but a *consequence* of symmetry. Read it as the bridge:
covariance is symmetric, so it always has a clean orthonormal eigenbasis, so a data cloud
always has well-defined perpendicular principal axes.

## Example 3 — diagonalizing a Hessian to classify a critical point {#sec-ex-hessian}

The second great family of symmetric matrices is **Hessians** — the matrices of second
derivatives of a scalar function (symmetric because mixed partials are equal; [GEOM-24]).
Consider the surface $f(x,y) = x^2 + xy + y^2$, whose Hessian is the constant symmetric
matrix
$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Diagonalizing $H = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ turns the surface, near its critical point at the
origin, into a sum of pure squares along the eigenaxes: writing $z = Q^\top \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}
x\\ y\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$, the quadratic part becomes $\lambda_1 z_1^2 + \lambda_2 z_2^2$ (the
full machinery of diagonalizing a quadratic form is [GEOM-17]). The **signs of the
eigenvalues** then classify the critical point — both positive means a bowl (local minimum),
which is the second-derivative test of [GEOM-24]. This is the spectral theorem doing the work
that powers second-order optimization.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hessian
#| fig-cap: 'The spectral theorem reads the shape of a surface off its Hessian. **Left:** contours of $f(x,y)=x^2+xy+y^2$; the level sets are ellipses whose axes are the Hessian''s eigenvectors $q_1$ (red), $q_2$ (green). **Right:** the surface itself, a bowl. Both eigenvalues of $H=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\\\1&2\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ are positive ($\\lambda=3$ and $\\lambda=1$), so the surface curves upward along *both* principal directions — a genuine local minimum. The eigenvalue is the curvature along its eigenvector.'
H = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(H); order = np.argsort(w)[::-1]; w, V = w[order], V[:, order]
q1, q2 = V[:, 0], V[:, 1]

gx = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 200)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(gx, gx)
F = X**2 + X*Y + Y**2

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.4, 4.2))
axL = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1)
cs = axL.contour(X, Y, F, levels=[0.5, 1.5, 3, 5, 8], colors=VEC, linewidths=1.2)
axL.set_aspect("equal")
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), tuple(1.4*q1), color=HILITE, label=r"$q_1$", label_offset=(0.25, 0.2))
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), tuple(1.4*q2), color=ACCENT, label=r"$q_2$", label_offset=(0.25, -0.2))
axL.set_xlim(-2.2, 2.2); axL.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$")
axL.set_title(fr"contours; $\lambda_1={w[0]:.0f},\ \lambda_2={w[1]:.0f}$")
axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2, projection="3d")
axR.plot_surface(X, Y, F, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.9, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y$"); axR.set_zlabel("$f$")
axR.set_title("both eigenvalues > 0: a bowl")
axR.view_init(elev=30, azim=-120)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"Hessian eigenvalues: {w[0]:.0f} (along q1), {w[1]:.0f} (along q2) -> both > 0 -> local min")
```

## Example 4 — the honest edge: clustered eigenvalues and near-degeneracy {#sec-ex-edge}

The spectral theorem is unconditional — *every* real symmetric matrix has it. So where is the
"edge of applicability"? It is not in the theorem's truth but in what the eigenvectors *mean*
when two eigenvalues are nearly equal. The theorem still hands you an orthonormal eigenbasis,
but the individual eigenvectors become **ill-defined**: when $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2$, any
rotation of $q_1,q_2$ within their shared (nearly two-dimensional) eigenspace is *almost* an
eigenbasis too, so tiny perturbations of the matrix — rounding, sampling noise — swing the
computed eigenvectors wildly while leaving the eigenvalues and the subspace stable. The
ellipse of @fig-ellipse becomes a near-perfect circle, which has no well-defined principal
axis. This is the honest boundary: trust the *eigenvalues* and the *eigenspaces*, distrust
*individual eigenvectors* when their eigenvalues are crowded.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability: near-degenerate eigenvalues. We take a symmetric matrix with two almost-equal eigenvalues ($\\lambda_1 \\approx \\lambda_2$, an almost-circular ellipse) and a clearly separated one ($\\lambda_1 \\gg \\lambda_2$). For each we add tiny random symmetric perturbations and overlay the recomputed top eigenvector (red) across 30 trials. **Left (separated):** the eigenvector barely moves — well-conditioned. **Right (near-degenerate):** the eigenvector spins around the circle — the individual direction is meaningless even though the theorem still holds and the eigenvalues are stable.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)

def trials(base, eps=0.02, n=30):
    dirs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        P = rng.normal(scale=eps, size=(2, 2)); P = (P + P.T)/2
        wv, Vv = np.linalg.eigh(base + P)
        v = Vv[:, np.argmax(wv)]
        if v[0] < 0: v = -v          # fix sign for a fair overlay
        dirs.append(v)
    return np.array(dirs)

sep = np.array([[3.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]])       # well separated: 3 vs 1
deg = np.array([[1.02, 0.0], [0.0, 1.00]])     # nearly degenerate: 1.02 vs 1.00

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.4))
for ax, base, title in ((axL, sep, "separated $\\lambda$: stable"),
                        (axR, deg, "crowded $\\lambda$: unstable")):
    th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
    ax.plot(np.cos(th), np.sin(th), color=GREY, lw=1.0, alpha=0.5)
    for v in trials(base):
        draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(v), color=HILITE, lw=1.4, z=3)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-1.3, 1.3); ax.set_ylim(-1.3, 1.3)
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.4); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.4)
    ax.set_title(title)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The spectral theorem always holds; the *interpretation* of individual eigenvectors does
not.** When eigenvalues are close, the eigenvectors are numerically and statistically fragile
— PCA on a dataset with two nearly-equal principal variances will report a "first component"
that is essentially arbitrary and that flips between bootstrap samples. The robust objects are
the **eigenvalues** (always well-determined) and the **eigenspaces** spanned by clusters of
near-equal eigenvalues (stable as a *subspace*). When you genuinely need stable individual
directions, you need *gap* between eigenvalues; the size of that gap relative to the
perturbation is governed by the conditioning story of [GEOM-26]. Reporting a single principal
direction off a near-circular cloud is a classic way to fool yourself.
:::

# Failure modes and the boundary of the theorem {#sec-failure}

The theorem is airtight *for real symmetric matrices*. The failure modes are all about
applying it where the hypothesis is not met, or over-reading what it gives.

**Non-symmetric matrices have none of the guarantees.** Drop $A = A^\top$ and every
promise can fail: complex eigenvalues (rotations), missing eigenvectors (shears,
defectiveness), or skewed eigenvectors with an expensive $V^{-1}$. If your matrix is not
symmetric and you need a decomposition that *always* exists with orthonormal factors, you do
not force an eigendecomposition — you use the **SVD** ([GEOM-20]), which is precisely the
spectral theorem applied to the symmetric matrices $A^\top A$ and $A A^\top$ that you *can*
build from any $A$. That is the right tool for the rectangular and non-symmetric world, and
it is built on this article.

**"Almost symmetric" is not symmetric.** A matrix that is symmetric up to rounding (e.g. a
covariance estimate computed in a way that left $\Sigma_{12} \neq \Sigma_{21}$ by $10^{-9}$)
should be *symmetrized* — replace it with $\tfrac12(A + A^\top)$ — before calling a symmetric
eigensolver. Feeding a slightly-asymmetric matrix to a general eigensolver invites tiny
imaginary parts in the eigenvalues and a worse-conditioned eigenvector computation. In code,
prefer the dedicated symmetric routine (`numpy.linalg.eigh`, not `eig`): it *enforces* real
eigenvalues and orthonormal eigenvectors and is faster and more accurate because it exploits
the structure the theorem guarantees.

**The eigenvectors are only unique up to sign and (in ties) rotation.** Even with distinct
eigenvalues, $q_k$ and $-q_k$ are both unit eigenvectors — sign is arbitrary. With repeated
eigenvalues the eigenbasis within the shared eigenspace is arbitrary (the near-degenerate
edge of @sec-ex-edge taken to its limit). Any pipeline that depends on a *specific* sign or
in-eigenspace orientation (some PCA-loading interpretations) must pin down a convention
itself; the theorem will not do it for you.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the spectral theorem the right tool, and when do you reach for something else?"* The
honest summary:

- **Use it** whenever your matrix is symmetric (and real): covariance matrices, Gram matrices
  $A^\top A$, Hessians, graph Laplacians, kernel/Gram matrices, correlation matrices. There it
  is unbeatable — guaranteed real spectrum, orthonormal eigenbasis, free inverse, clean
  geometry — and it is the foundation of PCA, quadratic-form analysis, and second-order
  optimization.
- **Do not reach for the eigendecomposition** when the matrix is *not* symmetric. For a
  general or rectangular matrix, use the **SVD** ([GEOM-20]) — it always exists with
  orthonormal factors and is the correct generalization. For a non-symmetric *square* matrix
  whose dynamics you care about (e.g. $A^k$), use the general eigendecomposition of [GEOM-15],
  accepting complex eigenvalues and a non-orthogonal $V$.
- **Beware the interpretation** when eigenvalues are clustered: trust the eigenvalues and
  eigenspaces, not individual eigenvectors (@sec-ex-edge, [GEOM-26]).
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You never form the characteristic
polynomial or solve null spaces by hand at scale — for symmetric matrices the routine is
`numpy.linalg.eigh` (or `scipy.linalg.eigh`), the "`h`" standing for *Hermitian*, the
complex cousin of symmetric. It returns the eigenvalues *sorted ascending* and the matching
orthonormal eigenvectors as columns of $Q$; it is built on the QR algorithm specialized to
symmetric (tridiagonal) matrices, which is faster and more accurate than the general `eig`
precisely because it can rely on the two guarantees we proved — real spectrum, orthonormal
eigenvectors. Two habits that follow directly from this article: (1) call `eigh`, never
`eig`, on a symmetric matrix, so you get real eigenvalues and an orthonormal $Q$ by
construction; (2) if a matrix should be symmetric but is not bit-for-bit, symmetrize it with
`0.5 * (A + A.T)` first. The reconstruction `Q @ np.diag(w) @ Q.T` returns $A$ to machine
precision — the operational form of @eq-spectral.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-15 *Diagonalization and the Eigendecomposition*]**, which gave the
general (and fragile) $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ and was honest that it needs luck:
enough eigenvectors, possibly complex, possibly skewed. This article showed that **symmetry
removes every defect** — real eigenvalues (@thm-real), orthogonal eigenvectors (@thm-orthogonal),
and the perfect factorization $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ (@thm-spectral). From here the trunk
continues into the quadratic-form world this decomposition unlocks:

- **[GEOM-17 *Quadratic Forms*]** — $x^\top A x$ for symmetric $A$; substituting $x = Qz$ and
  using @eq-spectral turns every quadratic form into a clean sum $\sum_k \lambda_k z_k^2$, so
  the eigenvalue *signs* classify bowls, saddles, and ridges. The diagonalization promised
  in @sec-ex-hessian is done in full there.
- **[GEOM-18 *Positive-Definite Matrices*]** — "$x^\top A x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$" turns out
  to be *exactly* "all eigenvalues positive," proved through this theorem; the good matrices of
  data science (valid covariances, convex Hessians) are the symmetric ones with positive
  spectrum.
- **[GEOM-20 *The Singular Value Decomposition*]** — the master factorization for *every*
  matrix, **built by applying this theorem to $A^\top A$ and $A A^\top$**: singular values are
  square roots of those symmetric matrices' eigenvalues, and the singular vectors are their
  orthonormal eigenvectors. The spectral theorem is the engine; the SVD is what it powers.

Downstream of the track, this article **feeds Unsupervised Learning's PCA** (principal axes =
eigenvectors of the covariance, @sec-ex-covariance) and **Inference's Fisher-information
matrix** (a symmetric matrix whose spectrum governs estimator precision).
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The name "spectral."** The word comes from the *spectrum* of a matrix — the set of its
  eigenvalues — a term Hilbert borrowed in the early 1900s while extending these ideas from
  finite matrices to infinite-dimensional operators. The finite real-symmetric case we proved
  is the cornerstone; the infinite-dimensional generalization (the *spectral theorem for
  self-adjoint operators*) is the backbone of quantum mechanics and functional analysis, where
  "$A = A^\top$" becomes "$A$ self-adjoint" and the sum @eq-spectral-outer becomes an integral.
- **Historical thread.** The diagonalization of symmetric (quadratic) forms by an orthogonal
  change of variables goes back to Cauchy (1829), who proved the eigenvalues of a symmetric
  matrix are real in the course of studying the principal axes of quadric surfaces — the same
  ellipse-and-ellipsoid picture of @fig-ellipse. The "principal axis theorem" of classical
  mechanics is this very result.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** *Why are the eigenvalues of a covariance matrix
  nonnegative, not merely real?* — that is positive-semidefiniteness, [GEOM-18]. *What is the
  analogue for a non-symmetric or rectangular matrix?* — the SVD, [GEOM-20]. *How fragile are
  the computed eigenvectors, quantitatively?* — eigenvector perturbation and the eigenvalue
  gap, treated through the condition number in [GEOM-26].
